text:
Admission Date:  1993-04-04              Discharge Date:   1993-04-04

Date of Birth:  1971-03-09             Sex:   M

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions

Attending:Tammy
Chief Complaint:
Non responsive, AMS

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Endotracheal intubation

History of Present Illness:
47yoM with unknown PHx and little OMR notes, who was at a club
earlier tonight that is apparently known for GHB. He was seen
earlier tonight to be dancing and normal. Then later found
sitting outside the doorway unresponsive. EMS was able to arouse
him, then was combative with spitting, kicking. FS 108.
Restrained then brought to ED where he was only minimally
responsive.
.
In the ED, initial VS: 97.4 71 163/90 22 98% RA. He was seen to
be snoring loudly. He then began vomiting into his airway and
was intubated for airway protection with Etomidate and
Succinylcholine and 7.5mm ETT, then started on Propofol. In the
ED they found two vials, one labelled "incense" and  another
vial of clear unlabelled liquid. Before intubation he had a
grossly non-focal neuro exam, with spontaneous movement of all
four extremities, PERRLA. There were no obvious signs of trauma.
CXR with tube in place, no evidence of aspiration.

.

Vitals before transfer: p72  162/99  rr19  sat 100% on AC
15x500, PEEP 5, FiO2 100%.


Past Medical History:
Unable to be taken at the time of admission

Social History:
Reported history of prior amphetamine use.  Denied prior GHB
use.

Family History:
Non-contributory

Physical Exam:
GEN: Young man, intubated and sedated. Bucking to pain and
manipulation of ETT but not responsive to commands.
HEENT: Pupils round, reactive to light with constriction
6mm->4mm on R and 5mm->4mm on L. Anicteric, MMM
RESP: Anterior and lateral breath sounds rhoncorous bilaterally.
CV: RR, S1 and S2 wnl, no m/r/g
ABD: Soft, NT, ND
EXT: no LE edema
SKIN: No rashes/no jaundice/no splinters
NEURO: See above- intubated and sedated with propofol, bucking
to pain and manipulation of ETT but not responsive to commands.
Spontaneous moving all extremities. No posturing.


Pertinent Results:
1993-04-04 02:05AM BLOOD WBC-13.2* RBC-4.55* Hgb-14.6 Hct-40.8
MCV-90 MCH-32.1* MCHC-35.8* RDW-13.2 Plt Ct-365
1993-04-04 02:05AM BLOOD Glucose-110* UreaN-26* Creat-1.4* Na-139
K-4.3 Cl-105 HCO3-22 AnGap-16
1993-04-04 02:05AM BLOOD ALT-26 AST-25 LD(LDH)-210 CK(CPK)-151
AlkPhos-71 TotBili-0.3
1993-04-04 02:05AM BLOOD CK-MB-7 cTropnT-<0.01
1993-04-04 02:05AM BLOOD ASA-NEG Ethanol-NEG Acetmnp-NEG
Bnzodzp-NEG Barbitr-NEG Tricycl-NEG
1993-04-04 02:30AM URINE Blood-NEG Nitrite-NEG Protein-NEG
Glucose-NEG Ketone-TR Bilirub-NEG Urobiln-NEG pH-5.0 Leuks-NEG
1993-04-04 05:39AM URINE Osmolal-902 Myoglob-PRESUMPTIV
1993-04-04 02:30AM URINE bnzodzp-NEG barbitr-NEG opiates-NEG
cocaine-NEG amphetm-POS mthdone-NEG

1993-04-04 05:49AM BLOOD Type-ART pO2-218* pCO2-43 pH-7.34*
calTCO2-24 Base XS--2

Imaging:
CXR:
INDICATION: Altered mental status, now intubated for airway
protection.
COMPARISON: None available.
BEDSIDE FRONTAL RADIOGRAPH OF THE CHEST: Endotracheal tube ends
5.4 cm above the carina. Nasogastric tube ends in the stomach.
Cardiac, mediastinal and hilar contours are normal. The lungs
are clear. There is no pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

Brief Hospital Course:
47yoM with h/o prior drug use found unresponsive, combative and
brought to ED where he was intubated for airway protection in
setting of emesis.
.
1) Altered mental status: He was intubated for airway protection
in the ED with concern for recreational drug use.  He his tox
screen was positive for amphetamines, and by history (after
extubation) he endorsed taking clear liquid at the club.  The
most likely drug is GHB given his history of agitation and
somnolence.  He pulled the ET the morning of his discharge.  He
had hoarseness, and a soar throat but otherwise felt fine.  He
remembered being at the club, and traveling to the hospital, and
some events in the ED prior to intubation.  He was discharged
without any physical complaints.

2) Elevated CR.  Of note, the patient had an elevated creatinine
upon discharge.  There was concern for rhabdomyolysis but his UA
was not positive for blood.  He was encourage to take adequate
PO upon discharge and follow up with his PCP.

3) Leukocytosis:  By history, the patient reported feeling sick
the last few days.  He had a mild leukocytosis at the time of
discharge.  He was instructed to follow up with his PCP if he
felt unwell.


Medications on Admission:
Unknown

Discharge Medications:
None

Discharge Disposition:
Home

Discharge Diagnosis:
Primary Diagnosis:
Altered mental Status due to drug overdose


Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.


Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. Barnes-

You were admitted to Unitypoint Health Marshalltown because you were found unresponsive
outside of a bar.  You were taken by EMS to the hospital were
you vomited, and were subsequently intubated.  You had a urine
test result that was positive for amphetamines.  You were
altered due to the small vial of (presumably) GHB you took last
night.  If you begin to fill ill, please contact your primary
care physician or go to an ER.  You were discharged with an
elevated white blood count and creatinine level.  You will have
have hoarse voice and soar throat for a few days since you were
intubated.


Followup Instructions:
Please follow up with your Primary Care Physician.



Completed by:1993-04-04
